text: The surgeon begins the procedure by addressing dense adhesions in the abdominal cavity, resembling the texture of bubble gum. The Roux-en-Y gastric bypass was performed in a retrogastric, retrocolic manner, and the initial step involves taking down the gastric remnant to access the right crus.

The surgeon navigates the plane between the liver and the inferior vena cava (IVC) to locate the right crus. Once identified, the surgeon proceeds with precise dissection to ensure adequate esophageal length within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon proceeds to the left side of the gastric remnant, identifying the roux limb marked by staples, and continues the dissection to locate the left crus.
The surgeon continues with anterior mediastinal dissection after locating the left cruz, encountering sutures from a previous hiatal closure that need to be removed.

The surgeon continues anterior mediastinal dissection to clear esophageal length, then moves posteriorly for additional length. Sutures from previous procedures are being removed. A surgical drain is visible around the esophagus for retraction due to the patient's latex allergy.

The surgeon uses robotic scissors to perform a combination of sharp and blunt dissection, carefully following tissue planes that are difficult to identify. The blunt dissection visibly opens up the tissue planes, revealing anatomical structures.

The surgeon uses fluorescent angiography with ICG to assess perfusion during the stapling of the proximal esophagus, confirming good perfusion visually. The distal part shows less perfusion under fluorescent light, which is not as apparent under white light.

The surgeon marks the transection line with a silk suture, which is also tagged to the diaphragm for positioning within the abdomen. A hands-on anastomosis is performed using absorbable monofilament sutures in two layers.

The surgeon performs the anastomosis over a fifty French bougie, taking big, full-thickness bites to ensure adequate tissue purchase. The bougie helps maintain the esophagus's diameter and position within the abdomen during manipulation.
The surgeon confirms adequate perfusion of the proximal esophagus using indocyanine green (ICG) fluorescent angiography, showing well-perfused tissue sitting nicely within the abdomen.
